planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 70°F; in zone 9 plant March–May. The cream-colored, football-shaped fruits should be left on the vine until the skin is hard and turns from yellow-green to deep golden-yellow. Cure harvested fruit at room temperature for 2 weeks, then store in a cool, dry place for up to 3 months. In zone 9 the long warm season allows fruits to cure fully on the vine before harvest.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Spaghetti Squash (also called Vegetable Spaghetti) is a unique winter squash whose cooked flesh separates naturally into long, thin strands closely resembling spaghetti pasta — when halved and baked cut-side down, the flesh is scraped out with a fork to produce a pile of mildly sweet, slightly crunchy, pasta-like strands. The mild, neutral flavor serves as an effective low-carbohydrate substitute for pasta under tomato sauces, pesto, or any pasta topping, and has become one of the most popular low-carb vegetables for health-conscious cooks since the 1970s. The oblong, cream-to-golden fruits (4–8 pounds) are a Cucurbita pepo type with the same excellent disease resistance and productivity as other C. pepo squash. In zone 9 the long warm season is ideal for spaghetti squash, which requires the full warm season to develop and cure properly on the vine.
sources: Cornell University Extension — Cucurbita pepo Winter Squash Varieties
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Winter Squash Production
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Pumpkin and Winter Squash Production

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is thoroughly warm; in zone 9 plant March–May. Charleston Gray is one of the largest home garden watermelons — fruits typically reach 25–35 pounds and the large vine needs generous space. Thump for a hollow sound and check the ground spot (which turns from white to creamy-yellow when ripe) to determine harvest. In zone 9 the long, hot summer is ideal for this variety, which thrives in heat and high-light conditions.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, sandy to loamy, fertile soil — watermelons are especially productive in sandy soils common to the Gulf South and Southeast; Charleston Gray is a 1954 All-America Selections award winner developed by the USDA in Charleston, South Carolina, and for several decades it was the dominant commercial and home-garden watermelon variety in the American South. The large, oblong fruits with distinctive pale gray-green skin and bright red, sweet, crisp flesh are resistant to anthracnose and fusarium wilt — the two most destructive watermelon diseases in the humid Southeast — which made Charleston Gray vastly superior to earlier varieties in southern garden conditions. The extra-thick rind (up to 1 inch) also gave exceptional shipping durability, which drove its commercial adoption. The sweetness and texture of Charleston Gray flesh represent the classic Southern watermelon flavor profile: intensely sweet, very juicy, with a fine-grained, melting texture that differs from modern seedless types. In zone 9 the heat and long growing season bring out the best flavor and maximum size.
sources: All-America Selections — Charleston Gray Watermelon 1954 Award
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Charleston Gray Variety Release
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Watermelon Production Guide

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Capsicum chinense requires warm soil (80–90°F) and is slow to germinate — use a heat mat. In zone 9 start in December–January and transplant in March–April. Aji Charapita is a short-day plant from equatorial Peru and may produce more freely with supplemental lighting in northern areas. In zone 9 the plant can be maintained as a perennial, returning to larger size each year from a woody base.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Aji Charapita is a wild-type Capsicum chinense pepper from the Peruvian Amazon, producing an extraordinary abundance of tiny pea-sized (3–5 mm) round yellow fruits on a prolific, heavily branching plant. The fruits are one of the most expensive peppers by weight in the world — sold as a specialty ingredient in high-end Peruvian and international cuisine for their unique combination of intense heat (30,000–50,000 Scoville), exceptional fruity citrus-tropical fragrance, and concentrated flavor. Because each fruit is pea-sized, a single productive plant can bear hundreds of fruits simultaneously, covering the plant in an impressive display of tiny yellow peppers. The flavor is described as intensely fruity with citrus, mango, and passion fruit notes beneath the heat — the signature Capsicum chinense fruity aromatic character concentrated into a miniature fruit. In Peru, Aji Charapita is used in ceviche, sauces, and as a table condiment in vinegar brine.
sources: New Mexico State University Chile Pepper Institute — Wild Capsicum chinense Varieties
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Specialty Pepper Production
Peruvian Ministry of Agriculture — Native Pepper Diversity

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May and again in August–September for a fall crop. Lime Basil prefers slightly cooler conditions than standard sweet basil and may bolt more quickly in extreme heat — providing afternoon shade in zone 9 summer extends the productive season. Pinch flower buds regularly to keep plants productive. The small leaves and compact habit make it well-suited to container growing.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil; Lime Basil is an Ocimum x citriodorum hybrid selected for an intense natural lime-citrus fragrance and flavor derived from high concentrations of limonene, citral, and related volatile compounds in the leaves — the aroma is strikingly and unmistakably lime, not just vaguely citrus as with some other basil varieties. The small, pointed bright green leaves have a fresh, bright citrus-lime character distinctly different from standard sweet basil (which contains primarily linalool and estragole). The lime flavor makes it a natural pairing for Thai and Vietnamese cuisines where lime leaves are traditional (dried Lime Basil can partially substitute for kaffir lime leaf), in fresh salsas, tropical fruit salads, seafood dishes, limeade, and cocktails. The compact, neat habit with small leaves is attractive in herb gardens. Like all basil, Lime Basil is frost-tender and performs best in warm weather, though it is somewhat more heat-tolerant than lemon basil.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops — Ocimum Species and Hybrids
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Basil Varieties for Florida
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Specialty Basil Production Guide

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or early fall; in zone 8–9 plant September–March. Running Tapestry is stoloniferous — it spreads by runners along the soil surface, rooting at nodes to create a weed-suppressing groundcover mat. The name 'Running' distinguishes it from clump-forming Tiarella types. In zone 9 provide consistent moisture and afternoon shade to protect from summer heat stress; mulch heavily to keep roots cool. Not suitable for zone 10+ where summer heat and humidity are too intense.

directions: Plant in shade to partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil with generous organic matter content; Running Tapestry Foamflower (Tiarella cordifolia) is a native North American woodland groundcover perennial that spreads via surface runners to create a dense, weed-suppressing carpet beneath trees and in shaded beds where most plants fail. The deeply lobed, maple-like leaves have distinctive dark burgundy-maroon markings along the veins that provide year-round ornamental interest — the pattern of dark veins against bright green leaves creates the tapestry effect that names this cultivar. In spring (April–June) the plant produces airy spikes of delicate white to pale pink foam-like flowers that rise well above the foliage and are highly attractive to early pollinators. Running Tapestry is one of the finest native shade groundcovers for the eastern and central United States — it naturalizes under deciduous trees, slopes, and shaded rock gardens while suppressing weeds far more effectively than mulch alone. The stoloniferous habit allows it to fill in completely within 2–3 seasons to create a low-maintenance, long-lived perennial groundcover.
sources: Missouri Botanical Garden — Tiarella cordifolia Plant Finder
North Carolina State University Extension — Native Shade Perennials
Pennsylvania Horticultural Society — Native Groundcovers for Shade
